quote: when my mom passed away, they Oh, Oh, I'm sorry. Different family members.
stewart_read: This is speaker_0 (the customer) disclosing her mother's recent death mid-search, then self-interrupting with her own 'Oh, I'm sorry.' The rep's nearest audible response is the fragment 'It's' at 06:21 — unintelligible, and almost certainly not an empathy acknowledgment. The rep has no audible utterance at the moment of disclosure. This is a more severe gap than wrong-execution: the disclosure received no response at all. The customer self-recovered and continued searching, which means the rep was spared the visible consequence — but the trust cost is real.
alternative_hypothesis: The rep may have responded verbally in a way the transcript did not capture clearly. However, the absence of any audible acknowledgment in the transcript, combined with the call's flat energy throughout, makes the reactive read (no response) more supported than the strategic read (response not captured).
coaching_implication: Show the rep the exact timestamp. The codex names this a 'trust gift' — the customer shared something personal and the rep had nothing. The coaching move is not 'be more empathetic' — it's: when a customer discloses a loss, stop the procedure, name what you heard ('I'm really sorry about your mom'), and then bridge: 'That's actually part of why locking in a fixed energy cost matters — one less thing to manage.' The bridge doesn't have to be elaborate. The acknowledgment is the move.

quote: I sent you a text, and then you can just send a picture of that back. And then I'll call you back whenever, and then I'll schedule you. Okay?
stewart_read: This is the call's structural failure. The rep exits with a soft callback — 'call you back whenever' — rather than a conditional booking with a locked time. None of the button_up required elements are present: no appointment datetime confirmed, no one-hour block stated, no phone appointment specified, no no-driving/at-computer anchor, no 'is that fair' handshake. The outcome is entirely dependent on the customer sending a bill photo and the rep remembering to follow up.
alternative_hypothesis: Strategic read: the rep may have judged that locking a time without the bill in hand would create a fragile appointment. This is plausible — but the correct move is a conditional booking ('Once I get the bill photo, I'll call you Thursday at 10 — does that work?'), not an open-ended callback. The strategic read doesn't hold because the rep didn't replace the button-up with anything structurally equivalent.
coaching_implication: Walk the rep through the conditional booking structure: 'I'll call you [specific day/time] once I have the bill — does that work for you?' This converts a soft callback into a locked expectation. The rep should also have sent the text during the call and confirmed the customer received it before hanging up — not as an afterthought.

quote: Okay. As long as you all own it and you're all in agreeance and you, own the roof as well, you're fine.
stewart_read: The rep correctly identifies three co-owners on title (Ernest and two sisters) but does not flag the appointment-inclusion requirement. Per the codex, co-owners on title must be present at the appointment — the same logic as spouse_decision applies here. The rep acknowledges the three-owner structure and moves on without establishing that all three (or at minimum Ernest, who holds the bill and is the primary occupant) need to be on the call.
alternative_hypothesis: The rep may have intended to address co-owner inclusion at button-up. But the call never reached button-up — and the co-owner inclusion requirement should be established at verify, not deferred.
coaching_implication: At the moment the rep confirms three co-owners, the correct move is: 'Since there are three of you on the title, we'd want all three — or at least Ernest — on the appointment. Is that something we can arrange?' This is a protocol, not a persuasion move. The rep needs to understand that skipping it creates a structurally vulnerable appointment even if the bill issue gets resolved.

quote: His mailbox is full, and he's not answering.
stewart_read: Ernest is unreachable and holds the bill. The rep's response is to ask the customer to search for a physical paper bill — which burns several minutes and fails. The codex's landline_protocol and alternative_paths section covers this scenario: when the bill-holder is absent and the customer cannot produce the bill live, the correct pivot is either verbal readback (the customer's husband had already provided an estimate of ~$140) or deferring bill collection to the specialist. The rep had a verbal estimate in hand at 04:16 ('he said, was around about 140') but did not use it as a bridge to schedule.
alternative_hypothesis: The rep may have needed the kilowatt-hour graph specifically (not just the dollar amount) and judged the verbal estimate insufficient. This is plausible for a paid lead. However, the correct response to that constraint is to explain it and offer a locked callback time — not to watch the customer search for several minutes and then exit with a soft callback.
coaching_implication: Show the rep the codex's alternative_paths section. The scenario here — bill-holder absent, customer has a rough verbal estimate but not the physical bill — falls closest to the landline_protocol pivot: defer bill collection to the specialist or lock a specific callback time. The rep should have said something like: 'That estimate is helpful — once you can get the actual bill from Ernest, text it to me and I'll call you [specific day/time] to get you scheduled.' The pivot needed to happen at 03:40, not at 07:29.

quote: My husband used to help take care of my mom's, the cove bill, he said, was around about 140.
stewart_read: The customer produces a verbal bill estimate (~$140/month) sourced from her husband's memory of the mother's account. The rep acknowledges it and moves immediately to roof type — no flip, no emotional anchor, no alignment-against-utility. Per the codex, bill capture alone is necessary but not sufficient: the bill must be flipped into emotional alignment. The rep had a number and walked past it.
alternative_hypothesis: The rep may have judged the estimate too uncertain (secondhand, from a deceased person's account) to anchor on. This is a reasonable concern — but the flip doesn't require precision. The pain the number represents is the leverage, not the number itself.
coaching_implication: The rep had a flip opportunity here. Suggested intent (not verbatim): acknowledge the estimate, then redirect to what that number means going forward — 'That's real money every month. The whole point of the appointment is to show you what that looks like if you could lock it in instead of watching it climb.' The codex's objection_inversion principle applies: the bill is the problem, solar is the alternative. The rep needs to make that connection out loud.

quote: What utility is that with, the the house in Harbor North? Do you know what utility it is? Because you should pay the bills up up there. How much utility is that? No. What's the company? Utility company. No. It's a Vepco. Is it Vepco? Okay. It's Vepco.
stewart_read: The customer is consulting a third party (likely her husband, audible in the background) to identify the utility company. This reveals that the household has active engagement with the property's bills and that multiple family members are involved in managing the account. The rep captures the utility name and moves on — but the background consultation is a signal that the husband is a present, engaged participant in household decisions, which matters for the co-owner/decision-maker picture.
coaching_implication: Not a failure moment — the rep got the utility. But the manager should note: the background consultation reveals the husband as an active participant in the property's finances. The rep could have acknowledged this ('Sounds like your husband knows the property well — would it make sense to have him on the appointment too?') to surface another potential decision-maker and strengthen the appointment's structural integrity.

quote: Yeah. It's a brand new roof, actually.
stewart_read: A brand-new roof is a strong positive qualifier — it eliminates the most common solar disqualifier (roof too old) and signals the property is in active, maintained condition. The rep captures it with 'K.' and moves immediately to credit. No acknowledgment, no energy built on a genuinely good signal. This is a moment where the rep could have shifted the call's tone from procedural to momentum-building.
coaching_implication: Show the rep what a momentum beat looks like: 'Brand new roof — that's actually perfect. That's one of the things that makes a property a great candidate.' It takes five seconds and changes the call's energy. The rep is sitting on positive qualification signals and not using them to build the customer's sense that this is worth pursuing.

stewart_read: The customer asks whether the rep has her email — suggesting she may prefer email follow-up over text, or is uncertain whether the text was received. The rep confirms ('Yes') and the call ends. The text_after_call codex requires a tieback to something specific the customer said. Given the mother's death disclosure and the three-owner complexity, a personalized text is especially important here — but the rep's exit gives no indication that the follow-up text will contain anything beyond generic appointment details.
coaching_implication: The rep should confirm the text was sent and read it back: 'I just sent you a text — it has my name and what we talked about. Once you get the bill from Ernest, just reply to that text with a photo and I'll call you [specific day/time].' This converts the soft exit into a trackable, personalized next step. The codex's tieback requirement means the text should reference the townhouse, the three co-owners, and ideally the locked callback time.
